summary: Target's drive-up and click-and-collect services are habit-forming and will structurally improve unit economics over time
summary_extended: Baker argues that Target's drive-up and click-and-collect services represent a structurally superior omnichannel model that is both habit-forming and increasingly profitable. He cites drive-up revenue growth of 734% year-over-year and an NPS score above 80, which compares favorably to Amazon's NPS of 62, suggesting Target is winning on customer satisfaction in last-mile fulfillment. Critically, Baker highlights that customers who use drive-up for the first time subsequently spend approximately 30% more at Target, indicating a powerful behavioral flywheel that expands the customer wallet share. The unit economics are also improving rapidly: digital fulfillment costs declined 30% YoY even as digital revenues nearly tripled, driven by a shift from expensive distribution-center-based fulfillment to lower-cost store-based fulfillment. The investment implication is a long position in TGT, with Baker describing the math as 'incredibly powerful' and viewing the model as a durable competitive advantage that will compound over the medium term.
detail: Drive-up revenue grew 734% YoY with an NPS score above 80 (vs. Amazon's 62). After a first drive-up experience, customers spend ~30% more at Target. Digital fulfillment unit costs declined 30% YoY even as digital revenues nearly tripled, driven by the shift from DC-based to store-based fulfillment.
confidence_evidence: Specific NPS, revenue growth, and unit cost data cited; Baker calls the math 'incredibly powerful'
quote_fragment: drive up revenues grew 734 percent year-over-year

ticker_audit.reasoning: The entire thesis is specifically about Target's drive-up and click-and-collect services, their unit economics, NPS scores, and customer behavior data. TGT is unambiguously the direct subject.
